items.correct: Every member of the target population has an equal chance of selection.
items.misconception: Just grabbing whoever is around (any haphazard method).
items.random_wrong: Picking participants who match the population profile.
items.why: Students treat 'random' as 'casual' or 'haphazard'. True randomness requires a defined sampling frame and a chance procedure (e.g., random number generator).

items.q: Asch's line study primarily demonstrates which type of conformity?
items.correct: Normative social influence (compliance to fit in).
items.misconception: Informational social influence (believing others know better).
items.why: Students assume participants genuinely believed the wrong line was correct. Asch's debriefs showed they knew it was wrong but conformed publicly to avoid rejection - this is normative, not informational.

items.q: When is deception in research ever acceptable?
items.correct: When demand characteristics would otherwise invalidate the study, with thorough debrief and ethics approval.
items.why: Students think BPS guidelines forbid deception outright. They actually permit it when justified, minimised, and followed by full debrief allowing withdrawal of data.

items.q: How many basic emotions did Ekman propose?
items.why: It's 6. “10” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
Correct answer: Found in all cultures. “Found in space” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: If 6 basic emotions, and 2 are 'positive' (happiness, surprise), what % positive?
items.why: Exact answer: 33. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
